data.title: ASML On The Verge Of Losing EUV Monopoly
data.description:
Investment thesis: Given the significant recent tech sector selloff, I have been looking at potential bargains, some of which I identified and already acted on. Despite a significant decline of roughly 40% from all-time highs, ASML (NASDAQ:ASML) is a tech company that does not look as attractive as one might assume, given recent news this month, such as that of Chinese breakthroughs on building their own domestic high-performance lithography machines. With Huawei testing its patented EUV machine this year and planning on mass production by next year, assuming all will go well, it seems that ASML's global monopoly on high-end chip-producing machines may be coming to an end. Given China's ability to mass-produce and do it cheaper, the market could potentially be flooded with Huawei's EUV lithography machines, as well as other competing products, faster than most might expect. There are also rising trade barriers that pose a risk to ASML's future sales prospects. Given its current valuation, the growing risks to ASML's prospects are far from accounted for by the market, which is why I currently have it as a hold, looking for a better initial entry point.

ASML is still priced for healthy profit growth, even though financial results suggest slowing revenue growth and lower profit margins.

ASML had strong revenue growth at the start of the decade, however, last year's results showed a dramatic decline in its growth trajectory.

For this year, its revenue guidance is between 32-35 billion euros, which suggests a re-acceleration even if it comes in on the low-end, unless it misses the mark.

For the last quarter of 2024, ASML's net income was about 31% higher than the same quarter from a year earlier. A net income of 2.7 billion euros on revenues of 9.3 billion euros amounts to a healthy profit margin of 29%. It is perhaps one of the best indicators of the benefit that ASML derives from its high-end lithography monopoly on the global stage. It should be noted that net income for the whole of 2024 was about 3.5% lower compared with the prior year.

Current liabilities increased by about 25% in 2024, compared with the prior year to over 20 billion euros. Its net interest expenses declined by 20 million euros year over year to just 20 million euros, which is an insignificant factor in its overall financial results, given its total revenues for the year of over 28 billion euros. ASML's low debt servicing costs stand out as perhaps the most attractive aspect of the company's financial results within the context of what might be coming in the second half of the decade. It is one of the factors that can help sustain ASML's share buyback program, which can offer share price support for investors, even as growth prospects may dwindle. It is also a solid financial foundation from which it may be considered to be in a good position to compete with an emerging Chinese tech ecosystem that does not include ASML, and it is supposedly developing similar capabilities, catching up to ASML's technological advantage in the field.

The technological containment strategy against China is a failure, and it may have made things worse for ASML.

When China released in "Made in China 2025" plan a decade ago, the realization set in that China would not be content with simply serving as the world's factory. It wants to have its place in the global tech ecosystem. The containment policy started soon after, starting with the takedown of Huawei, which the first Trump administration felt it would have a significant impact, which it did in the short term.

Huawei retreated for a while, and it seemed that it would just fade away, but with help from the Chinese government, it became an important platform for China's overall strategy of becoming either an important participant in the global tech ecosystem or a competitor to the current Western-dominated one. Last year, it returned to China's domestic smartphone market, clawing back its market share from the likes of Apple (AAPL) and others. It seems that by this time next year, it is planning to offer a viable alternative to ASML's EUV machines if all goes according to plan.

It is important to remember that resolving the high-end chip production capacity issue is just a small part of China's larger plan to build a global challenger to the current technological ecosystem. Parallel with these efforts, it came up with what seems to be an overall better AI tool in terms of costs & efficiency, in the form of Deep Seek and other domestic advances in the broader IT field. These advancements are designed to be integrated and packaged into end products for consumers. In other words, Huawei hardware may come with DeepSeek embedded in the product, as it will probably be the case with most domestic Chinese communication devices. What this may all mean for ASML is that consumers around the World who might want to use AI services, such as DeepSeek, will probably opt for Chinese-made devices that will be compatible with such programs. Those Chinese devices will increasingly not use chips manufactured with ASML machines, but rather Chinese ones.

The integrated global market scenario.

The process of technological decoupling has been a concept explored for some years now. It generally ends with the same conclusion each time, namely that it will have a net negative impact on the global economy. As I pointed out already, it is also a net negative for tech companies like ASML, since it hypothetically restricts future access and opportunities to the Chinese and other markets. It is questionable whether ASML and other Western-based tech companies would benefit if the complete splitting of the world into two separate tech ecosystems is averted.

Much still depends on how far along the likes of Huawei and others are in terms of challenging ASML's near monopoly in the high-end segment of chipmaking equipment. If reports of Huawei being on the verge of potentially mass-producing its own EUV machines as early as next year will prove to be true, this might be the last year that ASML will hold a monopoly on advanced EUV technology. Next year, its sales can indirectly be impacted by the presence of Huawei's machines, as companies that currently buy those EUV machines will brace for lower global sales. Thus they will scale back investments. Further down the line, Huawei & ASML machine sales may come into competition with each other in existing as well as proliferating markets, such as India. Profit margins, which are healthy for ASML at the moment, are likely to diminish as it will attempt to support sales and maintain global market share by lowering prices.

Investment implications:

  • Not all of ASML's sales come from EUV machines; older tech sales are holding up alright, despite growing competition worldwide.

As the data points above show, net sales are still dependent on China to a great extent, and less than half of its sales are EUV machines. Contrary to commonly held notions, most of the world's chip demand is not for the high-end top-of-the-line chips, but rather for chips produced with older capacities that end up in cars, airplanes, missiles, and other bulkier products. ASML is still competitive in the lower-end DUV segment, but there is already much competition around the world that offers alternatives, therefore, its growth prospects in that segment are increasingly limited.

Looking at ASML's sales by market, China is an important customer, despite current sales restrictions in place for the high-end EUV machines. Besides Huawei's EUV breakthrough, there is also a company linked to Huawei, called SiCarrier that is rising as a potentially dominant company in China's DUV market. It seems that next year will be a crucial one for ASML, because Chinese competitors in China, its largest market by far in 2024, are looking to produce a strong challenge to its market share. Beyond next year, things can potentially become a lot worse.

  • Risks to my thesis.

If most of the headline-making advances that are being trumpeted out of China will prove to be exaggerations, we are likely to find out in the next year or two at the most. In that case, my strategy to wait out a further decline in ASML's stock price might prove to be an opportunity missed. At the same time, an investment timed to coincide with a lower P/E ratio, which is what I am aiming for, may prove to be risky. In a worst-case scenario, Chinese competitors will exceed all expectations in all ways, and as a result, they might flood the Chinese as well as the global markets with their equipment, sooner and faster than one might expect.

  • Realistic expectations may produce a potentially lucrative investment opportunity if well-timed.

Because of the negative risks associated with growing Chinese competition, as well as perhaps a less robust global economy for the foreseeable future, ASML's current valuation, with a P/E of around 26, seems out of place with logical risk/reward considerations, despite the roughly 40% decline in its share price.

Between the rising domestic capabilities of Chinese companies to match ASML technologically, and the deteriorating global trade environment, ASML could potentially lose the Chinese market, which is currently its most important market by far. This is the risk that needs to be priced into its valuation and share price, which is yet to be fully priced in.

I am looking for a buying opportunity if ASML's P/E ratio drops below 20. It would be a good initial entry point, with an intent to add more if it drops further toward 15. At the moment, with a steep drop in its share price already on the books, instinct would dictate that a bet on an upswing may be in the cards. It may be the case in the shorter term, but as the trade issues and the China tech advancement issues get further crystallized, and with it the implications for ASML, there is a good chance that we will see further significant downside for its share price. Once these risks are priced in, ASML can become a buy, on its sophisticated technology that as of right now is still ahead of all competition, a solid market in the shape of the US-dominated alliance of Western and other mostly developed nations, where Chinese technologies are likely to be mostly kept out of the market, as well as a solid financial base. For now, I intend to keep watch, looking for the opportunity to buy in at a lower valuation level to arise. If it does not, I am willing to live with not having invested. ASML is a solid company, but the growing risks cannot be ignored.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PPLT: Gold Has Been On Fire This Year, Platinum Might Be Next
data.description:
Thesis

Platinum is a precious metal alongside gold and silver, and is commonly known for its high durability, resistance to corrosion, and use in jewelry and electronics. Precious metals have been on a tear in the past year, with gold and silver posting eye-watering returns:

The SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) is up 38% in the past year, while the iShares Silver Trust ETF (SLV) is up 33.9%. These are astounding figures, especially when considering the backdrop of high rates. Since metals do not produce any sort of yields, high interest rate environments are usually correlated with downside pressure on precious metals.

In today's article we are going to explore the abrdn Physical Platinum Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:PPLT), go through its structure and composition, and articulate why platinum might follow the same path as gold as silver.

Fund composition

Unlike other commodity ETFs which employ futures, PPLT is backed by actual physical platinum held by a custodian, in this case ICBC Standard. The physical vaults are located in London, and the fund actually has a list with the platinum bars they hold, including serial numbers and origin.

The issue with many commodities is that they cannot be physically held due to various constraints. Some are perishable (think agricultural commodities), while others have significant physical constraints (think oil and natural gas). Precious metals, however, do not exhibit this issue, thus physically backed ETFs are possible. The utilization of a physically held asset eliminates any futures linked issues around rolling contracts or market dynamics (contango versus backwardation, etc.).

The physical aspect of the holdings might also be appealing to investors who distrust the financial system, loathing investment banks and the counterparty risk inherent in many derivative transactions.

What is platinum actually used for?

Although it is categorized as a precious metal, platinum has real life utilizations:

Most of the utilization for platinum is in the auto industry and the jewelry sector. Platinum is a known component of combustion engine catalytic converters, while it also serves many functions in jewelry composition. Kindly note that while historically, it was also held for investment, that figure decreased in 2022 as interest rates rose.

The move toward electric vehicles is well documented, but the ultimate death of the conventional car is very far away. In fact, we are witnessing a transition to hybrid vehicles as we speak, rather than pure EVs. From this lens, the death of the demand from the auto sector is a decade away, if not more as far as platinum is concerned.

In the near term future, however, we are of the opinion that the investment category is going to perk back up, consistent with its historic pattern prior to rates moving higher in 2022/2023.

Correlations

While undoubtedly gold is the preferred store of value for central banks, silver has seen a substantial bid as well on the back of said gold flows. In this section, we are going to explore platinum's historic correlations to gold and silver, and try to imply if PPLT can follow the same parabolic move as GLD.

Looking back from 2010 onwards, we get the following correlations:

PPLT has a similar correlation to GLD and SLV, lingering around the mid 60% figures. From an annual standpoint, we can see a clear pattern:

The metal maintained its high mid-60s correlation for long periods of time, up until the Covid crisis. It then reverted to its historic levels, only to lag significantly the recent gold and silver rally.

Let us look at returns by year to get a sense of what happened during Covid and then more recently:

All metals were up during Covid, but PPLT lagged the monster move in gold and silver. The lower correlation therefore was due to a less positive move rather than anything else. Kindly keep in mind that while platinum is a store of value, during Covid it was affected by the significant decrease in auto demand, an aspect which can be seen from the demand table above as well.

Historically, the three metals are well correlated, with negative total returns during the same years, outside of 2024, when platinum was the only one lagging. If history is any guide, platinum is set to catch up on the monster move in the other metals from 2023 to 2025:

  • GLD total return: 58%
  • SLV total return: 36%
  • PPLT total return: -8.6%

If we use the same correlation factor as the historic one (i.e., roughly 60%), we should get an implied move of +34% using gold, and +21% using silver. Platinum therefore has a lot of 'catching up' to do.

Geopolitics and precious metals

One thing an investor has to be very cognizant of is geopolitics. Gold as a classic store of value is a trade which has been sparked by geopolitics. The Russian invasion of Ukraine and continued China - U.S. tensions play a very large role here. After Russian's actions, its central bank reserves held in dollars and euros have been frozen. While so far, only the interest due on those reserves has been taken and given to Ukraine, the entire reserves might be used to rebuild Ukraine post-conflict.

This state of affairs has made one aspect crystal clear for central banks - dollar and euro denominated assets are not necessarily a 'safe' store of value. Since said obligations can only clear through local clearing houses, they are subject to seizures under U.S. and European law. The only other clear-cut store of value is represented by precious metals, which can be physically held in Russia or China or any other country and serve as collateral for borrowings or be sold. Physical precious metals cannot be seized in the same fashion as other financial assets. While platinum is not as wide spread in the central bank community, it nonetheless possesses the same store of value qualities as its brethren gold and silver.

I am not a metals investor. Should I still consider PPLT?

The name of the game is to make money. Macro allocators are very good at identifying trends and moving to those pockets that generate gains during specific years. In equities land, there are momentum ETFs that do just that - they identify single name equities with the highest momentum, and add them to their portfolios.

When gold and silver have such significant positive momentum and platinum has a high correlation to those metals but has been lagging, the normal question to ask is whether platinum will follow. It is hard to time markets, thus calling for a top in gold or silver is very difficult to do. What is not difficult is identifying that platinum is historically cheap versus these two, and if history is any guide, it might need to do some 'catching-up'. In the same train of thought, even central banks might consider gold and silver expensive and might want to look at loading up on other physical precious metals, thus adding a bid for platinum.

Conclusion

PPLT is an exchange-traded fund that holds physical platinum. The metal is held in London vaults by a custodian, and thus the ETF is backed by a physical asset rather than futures. Platinum has had a strong historic correlation to gold and silver, but has lagged since 2023. A reversion to its historic correlation would imply a 20% plus up-move from here, given the gigantic rally in gold and silver in the past year. We are buyers of PPLT at the current price levels.

data.title: General Dynamics: Slow Growth With A Potential Market Change
data.description:
General Dynamics (NYSE:GD) is a company I have had on my radar for a long time, especially after their stock price has fallen by 10.22% through the past 6 months. In my eyes, this company is a recession-proof one, providing decent and steady growth, especially thanks to their defense revenue. But what if I were to believe that in the future, there will be much less conflict than there is now? Today, I have to rate the company as a HOLD, as I believe that in the far future, there will be much less conflict than there is now, as humanity matures.

Now to fully understand why my rating is as such, we will first make a market overview and check out the company's most recent Q4'24 highlights, to then analyze its fundamentals through our 9 Pillar Analysis, and finally calculate the company's intrinsic value through 4 of our intrinsic valuation models.

Introduction

First of all, I want to introduce what GD actually does. 53.00% of the company's estimated revenue comes from sales in defense, with the remaining 47.00% coming from Intelligence, state, and federal agencies, and commercial, with a 20.00%, 23.00% and 4.00% revenue share, respectively.

This is something I very much like about the company, as it is not purely dependent on a single sector. Furthermore, GD also operates in quite a few segments, with very evenly spread out revenue-generating abilities between them. Around 21.30% of the company's revenue comes from aerospace, both defense and civilian; 30.80% comes from marine systems, 19.00% comes from combat systems, and 28.90% comes from technologies.

One thing that can be taken both positively and negatively though is the geographical revenue split. In 2024, 83.80% of the company's revenue came from the USA, which on one hand is great, as it means that with the current Trump administration, it wouldn't be subject to tariffs. On the other hand, it means that most of the company's revenue-generating abilities aren't expanding past the US, and so if the US were to cut on military spending, which Trump has already proposed, and some cuts were already carried out, GD might experience a fall in its revenue, especially as more than 50.00% of its revenue comes from defense sales in the US.

Analysis

Now, as I am a long-term value investor, I like to look at things from an investing perspective of at least 30 years. Of course, in my eyes there will always be a need for defense, but in the long term, there might be less need than you think. Below you can see a graph depicting the battle death rates throughout the world declining quite drastically through the past 50+ years, even though since 1950, the world population has more than tripled.

Think about it: as less developed countries, such as some in Africa or some in the Middle East, which are now the main bearers of conflict, start becoming more developed, at some point reaching strong democracy like we have in Europe and the US, there will be much less demand and need for weaponry.

If you think about the two great wars we have had in the past 110 years, they were both between now democratic states, which we now wouldn't ever imagine fighting between each other ever again.

If my thesis were to be correct, in the next 50 years or even sooner, throughout the whole world, there would be almost no conflicts, erasing the need for most weaponry such as jets, battleships, submarines, etc. But again, that is just my speculation and my perspective. As already stated, defense makes up about half of the company's total revenue, meaning that if there was a decrease in the demand for weaponry, they would still have half of their revenue-generating assets. Furthermore, this huge change would be very gradual, taking decades, and so I believe that the company would take the correct steps towards shifting their focus to more civil machinery.

Q4'25 Earnings

But let's now throw my speculations behind us and take a look at the company's latest advances.

They reported their EPS as a beat by $0.12. It was expected at $4.03 and came in at $4.15. Regarding the revenue, that was as well a beat by $0.52B, reported at $13.34B while the expected revenue was $12.82B.

Now regarding the change in specific segments of the company, the Aerospace revenue grew by 30.00% upon the launch of the G700, with the aircraft sales up by 36.80% and services up by 17.40%.

The revenue from combat systems also grew YOY by 9.00%, with the Marine systems growing 15.00% and the Technology segment growing by 1.58%. So it is quite obvious that the company is prospering, with most of their segments growing at high single, to low double digits YOY.

9 Pillar Analysis

But as I have already mentioned, I am a long-term investor, so to really understand how well the company has been doing, and how well they are set to do in the future, let's jump to the 9 Pillar Analysis and check out their 5Y Fundamentals.

First things first, if you aren't familiar with the 9 pillars, let me quickly summarize this simple analysis model. Pillars 1 and 2 represent the 5-year Price-to-Earnings (PE) and Price-to-Free Cash Flow (P/FCF) ratios, respectively. Pillars 3, 4, and 5 illustrate the growth in revenue, net income, and free cash flow over the past 5 years. Pillar 6 focuses on the 5-year trailing profitability, while Pillar 7 highlights the change in shares outstanding. Pillar 8 tracks dividend growth, and Pillar 9 calculates the ratio of long-term liabilities to 5-year free cash flow minus dividends, which assesses a company's capacity to pay down debt within a reasonable period while still paying dividends.

Above, you can see that GD gets a decent rating of 8/9, with almost all the pillars sitting in the green.

Financial Growth (Pillars 3, 4 and 5)

First, let's check out the three most important metrics, which are, in my eyes, revenue, net income and FCF growth. Over the past 5Y, the revenue has grown from $37.93B in 2020 to $47.72B in 2024, accounting for a CAGR of 4.80%.

Regarding the net income, that has been growing at a 5Y CAGR of 3.76%, increasing from $3.17B in 2020, to $3.78B in 2024. The EPS has also increased from $11.00 to $13.63 in the same time frame.

Finally, regarding the FCF, that has increased by 12.64% in the past 5 years, growing at a CAGR of 3.85% from $2.83B to $3.19B. So overall, you can see that GD is a slow grower, with their average financial CAGR sitting at 4.13% over the past 5Y. This gives us a general idea about how much the share price based on value should be growing on a yearly basis. But to understand better how the company is valued, let's take a look at pillars 1 and 2.

Valuation (Pillars 1 and 2)

Regarding the 5Y PE ratio, it is sitting quite low at only 17.04 but has been growing at a CAGR of 4.80%, which means that investors have been paying more and more for the company's earnings through the past 5 years, with the PE currently sitting at 20.11, the highest it has sat at through the previously mentioned time period.

Regarding the P/FCF, it has been moving very similarly to the PE, with the 5Y average sitting at 15.85, growing at a CAGR of 4.78%, to now sit at 19.88.

So overall, I wouldn't say that GD is overvalued compared to their financials, as their PE and P/FCF are both sitting in decent ranges, but I do believe that they are gaining more and more premium from investors, which, when kept increasing at its historical rate, might make the company overvalued in the future and possibly lead to its stock price decreasing.

Debt (Pillar 9)

Now, one pillar I find extremely important is Pillar 9, which allows us to see how much LTL a company has compared to the FCF they generate. GD's LTL/5Y FCF - Dividends ratio is sitting at a decent size of 1.35, meaning that the company does generate enough FCF to cover all their LTL in a safe amount of time while paying out their dividends.

Something I also like is the fact that GD has been decreasing their LTL at a CAGR of 4.05%, allowing them to, in the future, possibly return more value to investors through dividends or share buybacks, as well as become more stable in case of possible financial downturns.

Overall, the 9 Pillars are looking quite well. GD does seem to be keeping their fundamentals healthy by decreasing their debt, while keeping their profit margin quite stable at around 8.00% over the past 5 years

Furthermore, their 5Y dividend CAGR is sitting nice and high at 6.60%, and the company has a 5Y FCF payout ratio of only 41.27%, which means that while returning more and more value to its investors, they also have safe dividends with excess FCF to push into innovation, raising their dividends further, paying down debt, etc...

The only thing that bothers me slightly is the company's slow-growing financials, compared to the fast-growing price per net income and FCF.

Intrinsic Value

So now, let's figure out with certainty if GD is over or undervalued through our intrinsic value models, starting with the Graham's Formula.

Here, I will plug in the 5Y EPS sitting at $12.85 and project a YOY EPS growth rate into the next few years at 6.33%. This will give me an intrinsic value of $197.71, 27.88% lower than the current price of $274.14. But we still have three more models to go through, so let's jump to the Multiples Valuation.

In this valuation, I will compare GD to two of its competitors: Lockheed Martin and Northrop Grumman. I will take these peers' PE ratios and average them out to 19.11, lower than the PE of GD. Therefore, when we multiply this average by the current EPS of GD, sitting at $13.63, we will get an intrinsic value of $260.43.

Next, I want to look at the DCF model, where I will project a 10Y + FCF CAGR of 5.70%, and combining that with a discount rate of 8.00%, get an intrinsic value of $229.42.

Finally, for the DDM, I will project a YOY dividend growth rate of 6.00% into the future and get a value of $318.00, higher than the current price.

To get the ultimate intrinsic value, we will average out the 4 values we just calculated and get $251.39, which is 8.30% lower than the current price of $274.14. If I were to also add a safety margin of 10.00%, our acceptable buy price would sit at $226.25, which supports my HOLD rating.

Conclusion

In conclusion, based on General Dynamics' slower growth and slight overvaluation, I have to rate them as a HOLD. This rating is further supported by my long-term thesis that in the future there will be less need for defense machinery, which might lead to the company struggling slightly. I believe that if GD was to drop further, reaching at least our intrinsic value, I might rate them differently, but for now, my rating is as stated above.

data.title: A New Chapter For United States Steel: Growth, Risks And Valuation
data.description:
Introduction

The United States Steel Corporation (NYSE:X) is a true legend of the American industry with a long history that began in 1901. Today, U.S. Steel produces almost the entire range of steel products: hot-rolled and cold-rolled products, galvanized, sheets, pipes. Recent years have been a roller coaster ride for U.S. Steel, to put it mildly. 2020 turned out to be an expectedly unprofitable year for the company, and it is clear why there is a pandemic. But already in 2021, the company made a spectacular return: $1.74 billion in net profit, and revenue jumped to $20.28 billion. The year 2022 continued its positive trend, but since the end of that year, everything has started to change again: the market has cooled, prices have gone down. In 2023, the profit was already $895 million, and in 2024 it fell to $384 million. The reason is another round of decline in demand and prices for steel, revenue fell to $ 15.64 billion. The pipe industry was particularly hard hit: the average price of products collapsed by almost 40%, and this hurt by the end of the year. In December 2023, the company decided to buy Japanese Nippon Steel for $14.9 billion, at $55 per share. The deal is still in the process of being negotiated, but the fact itself speaks volumes — even in the downturn, this company remains strategically valuable for the global steel market.

Business model

By the end of 2024, the company's business is structured in four areas: these are Flat-Rolled — classic blast furnace plants in the USA, Mini Mill — more technologically advanced and efficient electric steelmaking facilities (primarily Big River Steel), Tubular — the direction of pipes for oil and gas, and the European division of USSE — the plant in Kosice, Slovakia, which operates on the local EU market.

In terms of revenue, the Flat-Rolled segment is still the main engine, accounting for approximately 61% of all revenue. This is all the sheet steel that automakers, builders, manufacturers of machinery and more buy. The Mini Mill, which includes the flagship Big River Steel, is still the second largest (about 13% of revenue), but it is already clear that this area is becoming a key one. Especially after the launch of the new BR2 plant in 2024. The European division, the Slovakia plant (USSE), brings in about 19% more, while the Tubular segment covers the remaining 6% by supplying pipes for oil and gas.

The main feature of the U.S. Steel business model is rigid vertical integration. In other words, the company extracts the raw materials itself, processes them and produces the final product. In 2024, it produced more than 22 million tons of pellets and 4.5 million tons of coke. This strategy greatly reduces vulnerability to price spikes for ore, coal, and other external raw material chaos.

The year 2024 turned out to be difficult for the company — all segments went into negative revenue. Flat-Rolled declined by 11% due to falling shipments, Mini Mill sank by 7%, and Tubular lost as much as 37% due to falling pipe prices. The European segment sank by 15%. In 2025-2026, we are likely to see an increase in the share of Mini Mill in the business structure, and the entire model will become noticeably more balanced: from the extraction of raw materials to the production of high-tech steel for the needs of the automotive industry, energy and infrastructure.

Competitors

U. S. Steel operates in the steel industry, one of the most volatile and competitive industries in the world. Global production, according to the World Steel Association, reached 1.88 billion tons in 2024, demand increased slightly by 1.7%, but the overall market mood remains subdued: the economy in developed countries is stalling, and China — which used to pull the market up — is now stagnating. The forecast for 2025 is an increase of about 1.2% (to 1.815 billion tons). In other words, the industry expects low growth in the near future (a global CAGR of 1-2%). Faster rates are predicted in some emerging markets: for example, outside of China, cumulative demand growth is estimated at 3.5% in 2024-25, with India leading the way (+8% in these years). But in developed countries, demand is increasing modestly (North America and the EU – about 1-3% per year), while in China it is stagnating and has probably reached a peak (zero growth is expected in 2024 and a decrease after).

Within the United States, where U.S. Steel has its main business, volumes account for only 4-5% of global output, but thanks to protective measures such as duties and quotas, the company retains a strong position — it is one of the three leaders in terms of volume, occupying 13-15% of the domestic market. On the global stage, of course, the scale of business is more modest — less than 1%, and here U.S. Steel competes with giants like China Baowu or Tata Steel (OTC:TATLY). Internally, the main opponents are Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF) and Nucor (NUE). The former, like U.S. Steel, operate on an integrated model and are strong in the automotive industry. But Nucor and Steel Dynamics (STLD) are already a new school: they make steel in electric furnaces, move quickly, flexibly, and can maintain margins even in difficult periods because they have lower costs.

U.S. Steel has some advantages over its competitors in the form of its mines, vertical business integration and a wide product range, but the high share of fixed costs, union pressure and the need to quickly catch up with EAF competitors create significant pressure on the business, especially against the background of investors' expectations to see a quick return on recent investments. To stay in the game, the company needs to move forward without stopping, because the market is overheated, competition is fierce, and only those who know how to adapt faster than others survive here.

Financials

In 2024, U.S. Steel's revenue decreased by 13% to $15.64 billion, reflecting weak pricing conditions and declining shipments across the board. Flat-Rolled sank by $1.14 billion amid falling demand from the automotive industry and a decrease in volumes of almost 900 thousand tons. In Europe, through the USSE, revenue decreased by $542 million due to the recession and cheaper products. The pipe business became the most problematic: Tubular lost $581 million, almost 40% of last year's level, as the sharp pullback in prices after overheating in 2022 hit this segment hard.

At the same time, over the past five years, revenue has ranged from $9.7 billion in 2020 to a peak of $20.3 billion in 2021, which gives a CAGR of about 4% per year. The company has not published a clear guidance for 2025, but management says conditions are improving, especially in the second half of the year, based on the introduction of BR2 and restoration in pipes and Europe. I am putting in my forecast revenue of $16.38 billion (+4.7% YoY), and on the horizon until 2030 — growth to $18.57 billion, that is, a CAGR of about 3%. This pace looks realistic, especially if the new facilities are fully operational and the market does not fall into recession.

In 2024, business margins came under pressure — EBITDA was about $1.2 billion with a margin of 7.7%, which is almost three times lower than in 2022. The reasons are clear: steel prices have fallen, and costs — especially in the Flat-Rolled segment — have remained high due to underutilization of capacity and inflation. Operating profit was almost zero ($287 million), and net profit was $384 million, which corresponds to a net margin of only 2.46%. Nevertheless, the company remained in the black — unlike in past recessions when it easily went into losses.

As for 2025, the company planned to end the first quarter in the red (adjusted loss of about $115 million), but recovery is expected from the second half of the year. I expect EBITDA of $2.1 billion (margin ~12.8%) and net profit of ~$918 million, which is more than double the 2024 figures. The main factors are an increase in BR2 loading, stabilization of steel prices and economies of scale. By 2026, the consensus is that margins could reach an even higher level, especially if the U.S. Steel will achieve the stated savings of ~$150 per ton at the new facilities, which will give it a chance to maintain 8-10% of net margin even without a market boom.

In 2023-2024, the company invested in new facilities and continued to return capital to shareholders, which led to an increase in net debt to $2.89 billion. At the same time, the total debt remained stable at about $4.25 billion, and the increase in the debt burden was mainly due to a decrease in the cache to $1.37 billion. It is important that the debt structure remains healthy: almost all of the debt is long—term, the short—term portion is only $95 million, and interest payments are well covered by operating profit. Debt load ratios have increased, but are within acceptable limits — Net debt/EBITDA is about 2.4x at the end of 2024. The liquidity is high: in addition to the cache, there is access to more than $2.2 billion of credit lines.

The year 2024 was the peak year for U.S. Steel in terms of capital expenditures — the company invested $2.287 billion, directing most of these funds to the construction of BR2, the development of the Mini Mill segment and the modernization of the raw material base. More than 70% of all investments went to new mini-plants, which confirms the strategic focus on transformation. The CapEx ratio to revenue was about 14.6%, which is significantly higher than the industry norm and reflects the phase of active growth. However, a new chapter begins in 2025: management plans to drastically reduce investments to $1 billion, which is more than half the level of 2024. This will bring CapEx/Revenue back to the 6-7% range and free up cash flow, allowing the company to start reaping the benefits of previously completed projects.

U.S. Steel's free cash flow has been very volatile in recent years. With the growth of the investment program in 2023 and especially in 2024, the situation turned around: profits sank, CapEx remained high, and FCF became negative — minus $1.37 billion in 2024. This deficit was covered by the accumulated cache. In 2025, CapEx is expected to plummet to $1 billion, while operating flow will begin to recover. I expect an FCF of about $950 million in 2025. Judging by the comments of the management, the company is ready to switch from the investment mode to the return on capital phase. If the market does not fail, free flow will once again become a stable source of growth and an opportunity to reduce debt or pay shareholders.

In 2024, the dividend per share was $0.20 with a yield of about 0.47%. In 2025, according to the market consensus forecast, the company will allocate about $45 million dollars for dividends, or $0.20 per share with a yield of 0.47%.

Valuation

The company's shares have been noticeably lagging behind market growth over the past year and have been in a narrow price range of about $30-40. I believe that stocks as a whole are close to their fair values and do not see any potential for their growth in the coming year.

Firstly, the company trades at a premium relative to its historical averages in terms of multipliers: the premium for P/E is 105%, and for EV/EBITDA it is 84.3%.

At the same time, the company trades near average values with peers in terms of P/E, P/B, P/S, EV/EBITDA multipliers, as can be seen in the table below. HPE looks undervalued relative to the average values among competitors: the company's P/E is 27.04x, with an industry average of 26.14x, and EV/EBITDA is 10.45x with an average of 12.54x. That is, in general, the company has no underestimation of peers, but there is also no overestimation.

In my forecast of the target price, I use three estimation methods: DCF (estimated weight – 50%), target value of P/E and target value of EV/EBITDA with weights (estimated weights of 25%). In the basic DCF valuation scenario, I assume a 2.0% growth rate in the terminal period and a weighted average cost of capital of 9.40%. I chose TGR based on the long-term global economic growth rate of 2-3% per year. The WACC of 9.40% reflects a high beta coefficient of 1.86x, which indicates the volatility of the company's shares relative to market movements. Equity risk premium is taken from the Damodaran table. To keep my estimate conservative, I also added 0.5% to the calculations as a specific risk premium. The current 10-Year Treasury Rate is used as the risk-free rate.

The target P/E multiplier for 2025 is 13.15x, and EV/EBITDA is 5.67x (took historical averages).

According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $42.26 per share, which is the same as their current market value. I would consider buying the company's shares at a price of about $35.

An important factor remains the potential deal to buy U.S. Steel from Japan's Nippon Steel. In December 2023, the parties announced an agreement worth $14.9 billion ($55 per share), but the deal is still under regulatory approval and is controversial within the United States. Since the probability of its completion remains uncertain, I deliberately do not include its implementation in my target assessment. At the same time, the offer price from Nippon Steel itself can serve as a guideline for the upper limit of fair value, reflecting the strategic interest in the company's assets, but the current estimate ($42.26) is based solely on fundamental indicators and a realistic business development scenario within the framework of the independent existence of U.S. Steel.

Key risks for U.S. Steel's business

The first and most obvious risk is the cyclical nature of the industry itself: steel has historically been sensitive to macroeconomic conditions. If demand subsides or prices fall, profits can collapse dramatically, even with a well-designed business model. In addition, U.S. Steel still relies heavily on old domain assets, which are difficult to "switch" depending on demand, unlike mini-mills. This creates a conditional "threshold" for costs: as long as steel prices are higher, everything is fine, but if the market goes into negative territory, the company quickly loses margins. This is especially sensitive in Europe, where U. S. Steel Košice operates in an environment of expensive energy and increasing pressure from the EU climate policy. The internal challenges haven't gone away either: The integration of new assets such as BR2 takes time, and any disruptions at the start of new productions or conflicts with trade unions can slow down the pace of transformation.

Key Growth Drivers for U.S. Steel

The “Best for All” strategy and the transition to a hybrid model with an emphasis on mini-mills are one of the market drivers. BR2 is not just a new platform, but the key to transformation.: as soon as it reaches full capacity along with the expansion of Big River Steel, the company's share of flexible and more economical production will increase, which should automatically tighten margins and stabilize operational performance. The same InduX™ is an application for participation in the high—margin segment of electrical steel. At the same time, strong domestic incentives are working in the United States — infrastructure investments through IIJA and the Buy America policy, which protect the market from imports and generate local demand. At the same time, vertical integration — its own mines, coke chemistry — gives the company the necessary stability in an era of logistical disruptions and supply instability.

Conclusion

The last 5 years have been very unstable for the company due to the volatility of steel prices: financial results have fallen sharply, then quickly grew back. The company's management strives to reduce the sensitivity of the business to steel prices by developing the company according to the new “Best for All” strategy, but it is impossible to completely reduce this impact.

The company's shares have been noticeably lagging behind market growth over the past year and have been in a narrow price range of about $30-40. I believe that stocks as a whole are close to their fair values and do not see any potential for their growth in the coming year. According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $42.26 per share, which is the same as their current market value, so I assign the company a "Hold" rating. I would consider buying the company's shares at a price of about $35.

data.title: TCW MetWest Low Duration Bond Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review: Tipping Point

For a Federal Reserve (Fed) that elected to keep benchmark rates unchanged for the better part of five quarters with a data dependent approach to shaping monetary policy, the decision to implement what could be viewed as an aggressive 50 basis point (bps) rate cut in the third quarter represented more than just a reaction to an accumulation of worrying data points – it marked a shift from the committee’s laissez-faire approach over the past fourteen months towards one of newfound urgency. Spurring this shift was demonstrable weakening in the labor market, highlighted by the nearly full percentage point increase to the unemployment rate from last year’s cycle lows to 4.3% in July. Additionally, a decline in job openings to multi-year lows, a reduction in the hiring rate, and downward revisions to previously reported nonfarm payrolls provided evidence that softening had extended beyond just headline indicators, putting the labor market, and by extension, the lifeblood of the U.S. economy, in a precarious position. Weaker employment conditions were met with a slowing in consumer spending, including a compositional shift away from discretionary items towards essentials as the implicit and explicit costs of lower household savings rates, increased credit card balances, and still-high financing costs weighed heavy. When combined with measured inflation that continued to decelerate towards the Fed’s long-term goal of 2%, the weakening macroeconomic landscape forced a decisive repricing of the U.S. Treasury yield curve as both investors and the Fed dialed up expectations for future rate cuts in 2024 and beyond. Yields reset drastically lower as a result, with the policy-sensitive 2-Year yield falling 111 bps while the 10- and 30-Year yields fell 62 and 44 bps, respectively, driving a steepening of the yield curve and bringing an end to the 2s10s inversion that had persisted since July of 2022.

Though enough to warrant a pivot in monetary policy, shifting consumer and labor market dynamics seemingly did little to deter risk sentiment, even after the volatility event in early August that was as much a result of offsides risk positioning as it was fundamental factors. Equity markets shrugged off this temporary downward repricing, with the S&P 500 Index positing a 5.9% gain, bringing the year-to-date advance to 22.1%. Broad-based fixed income performance was similarly positive, evidenced by a 5.2% gain for the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index that helped bring year-to-date returns out of negative territory to settle at 4.5%, though much of this return was driven by the interplay of fixed income duration and a lower yield environment. Corporate credit was certainly a beneficiary of this dynamic as investment grade and high yield bonds gained 5.8% and 5.3%, respectively, though the latter outperformed on a duration-adjusted basis given broader risk-on sentiment and resultant narrowing of credit spreads to near the tightest levels of the decade. In securitized markets, agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) led with a 5.5% advance on the return of bank demand and steepening of the yield curve, while the non-agency sector benefitted from strong fundamentals, sustained demand, and favorable supply technicals. Meanwhile, commercial MBS gained 4.7% amid renewed activity and strong performance of private label deals, while asset-backed securities returned 3.4% on continued robust new issue sponsorship and sustained investor demand for the attractive yields offered across the sector.

Performance

Supported by lower yields, the TCW MetWest Low Duration Bond Fund I-Class (“Fund”) gained 3.76% (net of fees) in the third quarter of 2024 to outpace the ICE BofA 1-3 Year U.S. Treasury Index by 89 bps, while the year-to-date return of 5.23% was 109 bps ahead of the Index. Duration positioning remained longer than the Index throughout the period, resulting in a significant boost to relative returns as rates reset lower. Lower rates and a steeper curve proved conducive to agency MBS performance as well, with the allocation to the sector contributing to outperformance. Elsewhere in securitized, the allocation to non-agency MBS contributed on an issue selection basis given solid performance over the quarter, while private label CMBS holdings were also additive given strong total and excess returns for the sector. Holdings remained focused on single asset single borrower backed by high-performing collateral and/or sponsors, helping to avoid some of the broader credit concerns in the sector that impacted conduit deals or SASBs with weaker collateral. ABS holdings modestly outperformed, with contributions from AAA-rated CLOs. Finally, the corporate credit allocation remained relatively defensive, focused on more defensive areas of the market. Positioning added modestly as short corporates beat Treasuries by 32 bps for the quarter.

Outlook & Positioning

Conditions in the labor market are likely to continue worsening in the coming quarters as employers have largely exhausted lower impact ways to protect margins without significantly disrupting their current workforces, including hiring freezes or cutting back hours. However, an inflection point seems imminent as reducing headcount becomes one of the last levers left for employers to pull as margins shrink. Compounding this challenge is a consumer that has become increasingly stretched and bereft of the typical tailwinds supporting consumption, including wage growth, excess savings, and economic incentive to refinance their current mortgage to free up additional monthly liquidity – something that is unlikely to materialize soon given the prevalence of low, fixed-rate mortgages. However, a look at valuations across financial markets reveals an investor base that either undervalues the likelihood of a potential slowdown, or commands relatively scant compensation for such risk – both situations that, in our view, warrant a disciplined allocation of capital and preservation of liquidity. The Fed no doubt is aware of the situation facing the U.S. economy given the commencement of the easing cycle, though a commitment to remaining data dependent (with a focus on lagging indicators at that) virtually ensures a committee that is behind the curve given the reactionary, rather than proactive, approach data dependency entails. As such, there is an increased likelihood that the 50 bps move, enacted to forestall or even prevent a recession, was too little too late. Expectations are therefore not only for additional cuts over the coming months and quarters, but for these cuts to come at a more rapid pace than what is currently projected by both the Fed and market participants, leading to a steeper yield curve.

With a normalization in both the level and term structure of interest rates expected ahead, portfolio duration ended the quarter 0.7 years long versus the Index, with an emphasis on the front-end of the yield curve. Sector allocations remain skewed towards securitized products, where current yield and spread levels present a more compelling relative value proposition versus corporate credit. Agency MBS, a virtually credit risk-free instrument given the government guarantee, currently offers more than 40 bps of additional spread premium compared to similar duration investment grade corporates, informing a sizeable position that is further supported by the sector’s liquidity. Though non-agency MBS doesn’t carry the government guarantee, the sector is bolstered by strong fundamentals including low loan-to-value ratios on the underlying properties, and steady home price appreciation that has resulted in substantial homeowner equity and incentive for borrowers to remain current on mortgage repayments, maximizing cashflows to the bondholders. Away from residential MBS, the negative headlines surrounding commercial real estate have waned this year, though pockets of weakness remain in certain property types. As such, positions in the portfolio are more targeted and emphasize trophy properties with steady net operating income and experienced sponsors. Meanwhile, corporate credit maintained a more defensive profile, remaining focused on industries like communications, non-cyclicals, and those not closely tied to consumer discretionary spending. Finally, ABS positions similarly reflect targeted allocations given the challenges weaker structures or those backed by unsecured collateral are likely to face in a slowing economy, with high quality CLOs and autos comprising the bulk of the exposure and smaller allocations to government guaranteed student loan collateral, single-family rentals, and data centers.

This material reflects the current opinions of the author but not necessarily those of TCW and such opinions are subject to change without notice. TCW, its officers, directors, employees or clients may have positions in securities or investments mentioned in this publication, which positions may change at any time, without notice. This material may include estimates, projections and other “forward-looking” statements. Actual events may differ substantially from those presented. TCW assumes no duty to update any such statements. All projections and estimates are based on current asset prices and are subject to change.

The performance data presented represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Returns assume all income items are reinvested. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data presented. Performance data current to the most recent month end is available on the Fund’s website at TCW.com. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate with market conditions. The value of an investment in the Fund, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than its original purchase cost.

1 Since inception return for the index reflects the inception date of the TCW MetWest Class I and Class M Share Funds, respectively. For period 3/31/2000 – 9/30/2024; 3/31/1997 – 9/30/2024. Not annualized if less than one year. ICE BofA 1-3 Year U.S. Treasury Index – A subset of ICE BofA U.S. Treasury Index including all securities with a remaining term to final maturity less than 3 months. The index is not available for direct investment; therefore its performance does not reflect a reduction for fees or expenses incurred in managing a portfolio. The securities in the index may be substantially different from those in the Fund.

Source: TCW, BNY Mellon

Investment Risks

It is important to note that the Fund is not guaranteed by the U.S. Government. Fixed income investments entail interest rate risk, the risk of issuer default, issuer credit risk, and price volatility risk. Funds investing in bonds can lose their value as interest rates rise and an investor can lose principal. Mortgage-backed and other asset-backed securities often involve risks that are different from or more acute than risks associated with other types of debt instruments. MBS related to floating rate loans may exhibit greater price volatility than a fixed rate obligation of similar credit quality. With respect to non-agency MBS, there are no direct or indirect government or agency guarantees of payments in pools created by non-governmental issuers. Non-agency MBS are also not subject to the same underwriting requirements for the underlying mortgages that are applicable to those mortgage-related securities that have a government or government-sponsored entity guarantee. For a complete list of Fund risks, please see the Prospectus.

Glossary of Terms

AAA Rating – Rating issued to investment-grade debt that has a high level of creditworthiness with the strongest capacity to repay investors. Agency MBS– The purchase of mortgage-backed securities issued by government-sponsored enterprises such as Ginnie Mae, Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. Asset-Backed Securities– A financial security backed by a loan, lease or receivables against assets other than real estate and mortgage-backed securities. Basis Point (BPs)– One hundredth of one percent, used chiefly in expressing differences of interest rates. Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index or “the Agg”– Is a broad-based fixed-income index used by bond traders and the managers of mutual funds and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) as a benchmark to measure their relative performance. Bond– A fixed income instrument that represents a loan made by an investor to a borrower (typically corporate or governmental). A bond could be thought of as an I.O.U. between the lender and borrower that includes the details of the loan and its payments. A bond has an end date when the principal of the loan is due to be paid to the bond owner and usually includes the terms for variable or fixed interest payments that will be made by the borrower. Bonds are used by companies, municipalities, states, and sovereign governments to finance projects and operations. Owners of bonds are debtholders, or creditors, of the issuer. CMBS (Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities)– A debt obligation that represents claims to the cash flows from pools of mortgage loans on commercial property. CLO(Collateralized Loan Obligations) – A special purpose vehicle (SPV) with securitization payments in the form of different tranches. Financial institutions back this security with receivables from loans. Collateral – Property or other assets that a borrower offers a lender to secure a loan. Core PCE Index– Measures the prices paid by consumers for goods and services without the volatility caused by movements in food and energy prices to reveal underlying inflation trends. Corporate – Of or relating to a bond issued by a corporation as opposed to a bond issued by the U.S. Treasury, a non-U.S. government or a municipality. Corporate Credit– A term that is used in written investment materials and commentaries to refer to a corporation’s debt or to the corporate debt market as a whole. Coupon– The interest rate stated on a bond when it’s issued. The coupon is typically paid semiannually. Dovish– An economic policy advisor who promotes monetary policies that involve the maintenance of low interest rates, believing that inflation and its negative effects will have a minimal impact on society. Statements that suggest that inflation will have a minimal impact are called “dovish.” Duration– A measure of the sensitivity of the price (the value of principal) of a fixed-income investment to a change in interest rates. Duration is expressed as a number of years. Rising interest rates mean falling bond prices, while declining interest rates mean rising bond prices. Federal Reserve(the Fed) – The central bank of the United States which regulates the U.S. monetary and financial system. Floating Rate– Any interest rate that changes on a periodic basis. The change is usually tied to movement of an outside indicator, such as the prime interest rate. High Yield– A bond that is rated below investment grade. Inflation– A condition of a rise in the general level of prices of goods and services in an economy over a period of time. Investment Grade– A bond that is rated Baa3/BBB- or higher by Moody’s, Standard & Poors and Fitch. Labor Market– The nominal market in which workers find paying work, employers find willing workers, and wage rates are determined. Liquidity– The ability to convert an asset to cash quickly. MBS(Mortgage-Backed Securities) – A type of asset-backed security that is secured by a mortgage or collection of mortgages. These securities must also be grouped in one of the top two ratings as determined by an accredited credit rating agency, and usually pay periodic payments that are similar to coupon payments. Furthermore, the mortgage must have originated from a regulated and authorized financial institution. Monetary Policy– The actions of a central bank, currency board or other regulatory committee that determine the size and rate of growth of the money supply, which in turn affects interest rates. Non-agency – Mortgage backed securities made up of mortgage loans that are not guaranteed by a government-supported agency. Non-Agency MBS– Mortgage backed securities sponsored by private companies other than government sponsored enterprises such as Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. Non-cyclical– Non-cyclical stocks repeatedly outperform the market when economic growth slows. Non-cyclical securities are generally profitable regardless of economic trends because they produce or distribute goods and services we always need, including things like food, power, water, and gas. Overweight – A condition where the portfolio exposure to a given asset class (or risk measure) exceeds that of the benchmark index. S&P 500- The S&P 500 Index, or Standard & Poor’s 500 Index, is a market-capitalization-weighted index of 500 leading publicly traded companies in the U.S. The index actually has 503 components because three of them have two share classes listed. Securitized– When a bank pools together different kinds of loans to clear them off its balance sheet, to free up credit for new lenders, and to create new securities that can be marketed and sold to investors. Spread– The difference between the bid and the ask price of a security or asset. Total Return– The rate of return on a security, including income from dividends and interest, as well as appreciation or depreciation in the price of the security, over a given time period of time. Tranches– Are segments created from a pool of securities—usually debt instruments such as bonds or mortgages—that are divvied up by risk, time to maturity, or other characteristics in order to be marketable to different investors. Each portion or tranche of a securitized or structured product is one of several related securities offered at the same time, but with varying risks, rewards and maturities to appeal to a diverse range of investors. Treasury Yield– The return on investment, expressed as a percentage, on the debt obligations of the U.S. government. Underweight– A condition where a portfolio does not hold a sufficient amount of a particular security when compared to the security’s weight in the underlying benchmark portfolio. Unemployment Rate– The percentage of the total labor force that is unemployed but actively seeking employment and willing to work.

U.S. Treasuries (U.S. Treasury Securities)– Bills, notes and bonds that are debt obligations of the U.S. government. Valuations– The process of determining the current worth of an asset or company. There are many techniques that can be used to determine value, some are subjective and others are objective. Volatility– A measure of the risk of price moves for a security calculated from the standard deviation of day to day logarithmic historical price changes. Yield– The income return on an investment. This refers to the interest or dividends received from a security and is usually expressed annually as a percentage based on the investment’s cost, its current market value or its face value. Yield Curve – A curve on a graph in which the yield of fixed-interest securities is plotted against the length of time they have to run to maturity.

For more information about the Fund call us at 800 241 4671.

Visit our web site for a full menu of products and services at TCW.com.

The TCW MetWest Funds are advised by Metropolitan West Asset Management, LLC, which is a wholly-owned subsidiary of The TCW Group, Inc.

data.title: TCW MetWest Unconstrained Bond Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review: The Economy – Breaking Down or Busting Out?

For an apparently fading economy that had sufficiently convinced the Federal Reserve to initiate monetary easing in September – with a 50 basis point (bp) cut, to boot – the market emphatically cast doubts on that view to close out 2024. Embracing the growth outlook, in an expression of dissent, broad equity indexes added to accumulated year-to-date gains while, more pertinently, interest rates sold off to move higher, especially out the yield curve. The summertime reflection that brought Fed Chair Powell to an ease was labor market weakness borne of decelerating job creation and rising unemployment, even as inflation remained sticky and above its 2% target. That rate “rebellion” started on cue from the September ease, with 5- to 10-Year U.S. Treasury yields up nearly 100 bps from mid-September to year-end, while the Fed eased two more times, delivering a “bear steepening” scenario. The bond market reaction was no doubt further abetted by an election outcome suggesting a more invigorated economy promising higher growth as virtue and potential tariff-induced inflationary effects as vice. Added up, though more of the rise DID come from the virtuous growth side of the ledger, it still meant for increased rates and generally disappointing bond returns as a result.

As a consequence of the higher rates, fixed income returns were negative as the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index fell 3.1% in the fourth quarter and culminated a likewise unspectacular 1.3% advance for the year. While shorter maturity indexes delivered a flat Q4, i.e., no-to-limited losses, almost anything carrying duration beyond three years fell into the red and ceded earlier 2024 gains. Relatively, corporates captured a better experience for both Q4 and 2024 as higher coupons and resilient yield spreads generated considerable excess returns (to comparable duration U.S. Treasury issues) over those periods. While investment grade corporate outperformance was clear and durable, though still negative overall in Q4 due to the duration drag, high yield scratched out not only a marginally positive 0.2% Q4 return but a respectable 8.2% gain for the year also. (As an aside, this “risk on” sentiment was reflected in more buoyant equity market returns captured by the S&P 500’s 2.4% fourth quarter advance and 25% year-over-year gain, its second straight 20%-plus year, a feat last occurring some 20 years ago.) Among securitized issues, agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) were also weighed down by higher rates to post a 3.2% Q4 loss, pulling the annual return down to a mere 1.2% gain, making it the laggard among the sectors. Non-agency MBS performed better for both the quarter and year against a backdrop of improving collateral fundamentals (mainly, falling loan-to-value ratios) and solid demand, while commercial MBS (CMBS) was similarly strong for the year with a 4.7% gain and good duration-adjusted returns, though lost 1.5% in Q4 given the rate moves. Asset-backed securities (ABS) provided the best returns among securitized products for 2024 with a 5% gain, owing largely to the floating rate issuance providing insulation from rate volatility and offering attractive coupons.

Performance

Challenged by a higher rate environment, the TCW MetWest Unconstrained Bond Fund I-Class (“Fund”) lost 0.64% net of fees in the final quarter of 2024 to trail the Bloomberg U.S. 1-3 Month T-Bill Index (“Index”) by 183 bps, while the full year return of 5.99% was 67 bps ahead of the Index. A recognition that rates at current levels remain restrictive and above equilibrium conditions informed the continued positive duration position, which was the largest detractor from relative performance in both periods. Turning to sector allocation, the impact was positive in both the fourth quarter and year, but much larger for the latter. Though higher yields over the quarter and year weighed on absolute returns, yield spreads compressed across fixed income sectors, resulting in outperformance versus duration-matched Treasuries in both periods. More significant tightening for the full year period led to a much larger contribution to relative performance, with the selection impact outpacing the duration drag and driving outperformance for the period. Looking at sectors more specifically, allocations across corporate credit remained defensive, but added to relative performance as credit spreads compressed to multi-year tights, providing opportunities to trim into strength and optimize existing holdings. However, securitized allocations contributed the most, driven by CMBS and ABS holdings focused on single asset single borrower (SASB) deals and CLOs – both of which helped the Fund carry an overall yield advantage relative the Index. Non-agency MBS was also a notable contributor for the year as yield premiums fell across the sector, though holdings were modestly wider over the quarter, resulting in a small drag, while agency MBS detracted over the year as rate volatility weighed on the sector.

Outlook & Positioning

Though the dominant theme across markets has been the re-emergence of a “Goldilocks” economy following the election and attendant reinvigoration of U.S. growth prospects, there remains potential for volatility that could challenge market conditions and pricing. For one, the labor market continues to cool and reflect sagging employment demand which, despite not yet flowing through to higher unemployment, has been evident in more leading indicators. Despite being seemingly lost among the recent market moves, TCW remains of the view that the labor market is the key factor driving the direction of the economy, and that the trend of weak at worst and mixed-at-best data portends challenges to economic growth potential. Second, valuations, most notably credit spreads, have almost never been tighter, meaning considerable good news is already embedded in prices, creating an interesting paradox in that there is little to no protection should things not unfold as markets expect – which is of course exactly when one would want that protection. In terms of those expectations, market pricing in November clearly indicated that investors project great things from the new administration…and immediately, judging from risk market pricing. However, policy implementation takes time, and any potential benefits are likely to be felt with meaningful lags and with potential volatile secondary effects that may be difficult to fully account for today. Lastly, the recent surge in Treasury yields has kept rates restrictive, i.e., above equilibrium, and at levels that are likely to come down over the intermediate and longer term due to slowing effects throughout the real economy, most notably in higher financing costs that weigh on economic growth and, of course, on those most in need of credit.

TCW remains committed to our time-tested value-oriented philosophy, with bottom-up portfolio construction and relative value sector allocations the pillars by which we introduce carry and return potential to portfolios. This is reflected in a reduced allocation to corporate credit given tight credit spreads, though with targeted positions in high-conviction issuers and industries like communications and utilities, the latter offering a variety of opportunities to capitalize on the structural tailwind of power generation and demand. Meanwhile, securitized products present a more compelling relative value proposition given wider yield spreads, informing a sizable allocation across sectors. Agency MBS represents a large position given favorable liquidity, the government guarantee, and over 50 bps of spread premium versus investment grade credit. Though non-agency MBS doesn’t carry the government guarantee, the sector is bolstered by robust collateral profiles that help maximize cashflow back to bondholders, with the ongoing evolution of structures and deal types across the sector fertile ground to capture alpha potential at the issue level. Similarly, CMBS exposure is focused on SASB deals that allow for a detailed analysis of the underlying property and sponsor, facilitating bottom-up inclusion criteria. ABS positions similarly reflect targeted allocations like senior CLO tranches and high-quality non-traditional collateral types like single-family rentals and data centers. Finally, the Fund’s duration position remained positive and on the longer end of the strategy’s typical range, finishing the quarter at 3.0 years given expectations for lower rates and a steeper curve.
